.
Which paragraph contains the following information?

, in boxes 14–16 on your answer sheet.

obj.title: Procrastination
obj.content:
[center]A psychologist explains why we put off important tasks and how we can break this habit[/center][br][br]
[p:a]Procrastination is the habit of delaying a necessary task, usually by focusing on less urgent, more enjoyable, and easier activities instead. We all do it from time to time. We might be composing a message to a friend who we have to let down, or putting together an important report for college or work; we're doing our best to avoid doing the job at hand, but deep down we know that we should just be getting on with it. Unfortunately, berating ourselves won't stop us procrastinating again. In fact, it's one of the worst things we can do. This matters because, as my research shows, procrastination doesn't just waste time, but is actually linked to other problems, too.[br][br]
[p:b]Contrary to popular belief, procrastination is not due to laziness or poor time management. Scientific studies suggest procrastination is, in fact, caused by poor mood management. This makes sense if we consider that people are more likely to put off starting or completing tasks that they are really not keen to do. If just thinking about the task threatens our sense of self-worth or makes us anxious, we will be more likely to put it off. Research involving brain imaging has found that areas of the brain linked to detection of threats and emotion regulation are actually different in people who chronically procrastinate compared to those who don't procrastinate frequently.[br][br]
[p:c]Tasks that are emotionally loaded or difficult, such as preparing for exams, are prime candidates for procrastination. People with low self-esteem are more likely to procrastinate. Another group of people who tend to procrastinate are perfectionists, who worry their work will be judged harshly by others. We know that if we don't finish that report or complete those home repairs, then what we did can't be evaluated. When we avoid such tasks, we also avoid the negative emotions associated with them. This is rewarding, and it conditions us to use procrastination to repair our mood. If we engage in more enjoyable tasks instead, we get another mood boost. In the long run, however, procrastination isn't an effective way of managing emotions. The 'mood repair' we experience is temporary. Afterwards, people tend to be left with a sense of guilt that not only increases their negative mood, but also reinforces their tendency to procrastinate.[br][br]
[p:d]So why is this such a problem? When most people think of the costs of procrastination, they think of the toll on productivity. For example, studies have shown that procrastination negatively impacts on student performance. But putting off reading textbooks and writing essays may affect other areas of students' lives. In one study of over 3,000 German students over a six-month period, those who reported procrastinating over their university work were also more likely to engage in study-related misconduct, such as cheating and plagiarism. But the behaviour that procrastination was most closely linked with was using fraudulent excuses to get deadline extensions. Other research shows that employees on average spend almost a quarter of their workday procrastinating, and again this is linked with negative outcomes. In fact, in one US survey of over 22,000 employees, participants who said they regularly procrastinated had less annual income and less employment stability. For every one-point increase on a measure of chronic procrastination, annual income decreased by US$15,000.[br][br]
[p:e]Procrastination also correlates with serious health and well-being problems. A tendency to procrastinate is linked to poor mental health, including higher levels of depression and anxiety. Across numerous studies, I've found people who regularly procrastinate report a greater number of health issues, such as headaches, flu and colds, and digestive issues. They also experience higher levels of stress and poor sleep quality. They are less likely to practise healthy behaviours, such as eating a healthy diet and regularly exercising, and use destructive coping strategies to manage their stress. In one study of over 700 people, I found people prone to procrastination had a 63% greater risk of poor heart health after accounting for other personality traits and demographics.[br][br]
[p:f]Finding better ways of managing our emotions is one route out of the vicious cycle of procrastination. An important first step is to manage our environment and how we view the task. There are a number of evidence-based strategies that can help us fend off distractions that can occupy our minds when we should be focusing on the thing we should be getting on with. For example, reminding ourselves about why the task is important and valuable can increase positive feelings towards it. Forgiving ourselves and feeling compassion when we procrastinate can help break the procrastination cycle. We should admit that we feel bad, but not be overly critical of ourselves. We should remind ourselves that we're not the first person to procrastinate, nor the last. Doing this can take the edge off the negative feelings we have about ourselves when we procrastinate. This can all make it easier to get back on track.
obj.content_zh:
[center]一位心理学家解释我们为什么推迟重要任务以及如何改掉这个习惯[/center][br][br]
[b] A. [/b]拖延症是指推迟必要任务的习惯，通常会把注意力转向那些不那么紧急、更愉快或更容易的活动。我们有时都会这样做。也许发生在给朋友写拒绝信息时，或是在准备学校或工作的重要报告时;我们尽力逃避手头的任务，但内心深处我们知道应该立即着手处理。不幸的是，自责并不能阻止我们再次拖延。事实上，这是我们能做的最糟糕的做法之一。这很重要，因为就像我的研究表明的那样，拖延不仅浪费时间，实际上还与其他问题相关。[br][br]
[b] B. [/b]与普遍看法相反，拖延症并非源于懒惰或时间管理不善。事实上，科学研究表明，拖延实际上是由情绪管理不佳引起的。如果我们考虑到人们更可能推迟开始或完成他们实在不愿意做的任务，这一点就说得通了。如果仅仅想到某项任务就会威胁到我们的自我价值感或让我们焦虑，我们就更可能拖延。脑成像的研究发现，长期拖延者与不常拖延者相比，大脑中与监测威胁和情绪调控相关的区域确实存在差异。[br][br]
[b] C. [/b]那些带有情绪负担或难度较高的任务，比如备考，最容易引发拖延行为。自尊心较低的人更容易拖延。另一个容易拖延的人群是完美主义者，他们担心自己的工作会受到他人严苛的评价。我们知道，如果不完成那份报告或那些家居维修，我们的工作就不会被评价。当我们在逃避这些任务的时候，我们也避开了与之相关的负面情绪。这种行为会带来即时满足感，使我们养成用拖延来修复情绪的习惯。如果我们转而从事更愉快的活动，情绪会得到进一步提振。然而从长远来看，拖延并非管理情绪的有效方式。我们体验到的"情绪修复"只是暂时的。事后，人们往往会感到内疚，这不仅加剧了负面情绪，还强化了拖延倾向。[br][br]
[b] D. [/b]所以为什么这是个严重问题?大多数人想到拖延的代价时，会联想到对生产效率的影响。例如，研究显示拖延会对学生成绩产生负面影响但推迟阅读教科书和撰写论文可能影响学生生活的其他方面。在一项针对3000多名德国学生的六个月的研究中，那些承认在学习上拖延的学生也更有可能发生与学业相关的不端行为，如作弊和抄袭。但与拖延关联最密切的行为是编造虚假理由来获取延期许可。其他研究显示员工平均每天花费近四分之一的工作时间拖延，并且这样会带来负面结果。事实上，在美国一项对22000多名员工的调查中，经常拖延的员工年收入更低，工作稳定性更差。在长期拖延上每增加1分，年收入就会减少15000美元。[br][br]
[b] E. [/b]拖延行为还与严重的健康和福祉问题密切相关。拖延倾向往往和较差的心理健康状况有关包括更高程度的抑郁和焦虑症状。通过大量研究，我发现习惯性拖延者反映存在更多健康问题，如头痛、流感、感冒和消化系统疾病。他们还经历着更高强度的压力和更差的睡眠质量。这类人群较少采取健康生活方式，如均衡饮食和规律运动，而是使用破坏性的应对策略来处理压力。在一项700多人的研究中，在排除其他人格特征和人口统计学因素后，我发现具有拖延倾向的人群出现心脏健康问题的风险要高出63%。[br][br]
[b] F. [/b]寻找更好的管理情绪方法是脱离拖延恶性循环的有效途径。关键的第一步是改善我们的环境和看待任务的方式。大量有证据基础的策略可以帮助我们抵御那些在应该专注工作时占据大脑的令人分心的事物。例如，提醒自己任务的重要性和价值可以增强对任务的积极感受。当我们拖延时原谅我们自己并且保持同理心可以帮助打破拖延循环。我们应当承认我们感到糟糕，但不要过度自我批判。我们要提醒自己我们不是第一个拖延的人，也不会是最后一个。这种做法能够弱化我们因拖延而产生的负面情绪，也让我们更容易回到正轨。
obj.html_content:
A psychologist explains why we put off important tasks and how we can break this habit


aProcrastination is the habit of delaying a necessary task, usually by focusing on less urgent, more enjoyable, and easier activities instead. We all do it from time to time. We might be composing a message to a friend who we have to let down, or putting together an important report for college or work; we're doing our best to avoid doing the job at hand, but deep down we know that we should just be getting on with it. Unfortunately, berating ourselves won't stop us procrastinating again. In fact, it's one of the worst things we can do. This matters because, as my research shows, procrastination doesn't just waste time, but is actually linked to other problems, too.

bContrary to popular belief, procrastination is not due to laziness or poor time management. Scientific studies suggest procrastination is, in fact, caused by poor mood management. This makes sense if we consider that people are more likely to put off starting or completing tasks that they are really not keen to do. If just thinking about the task threatens our sense of self-worth or makes us anxious, we will be more likely to put it off. Research involving brain imaging has found that areas of the brain linked to detection of threats and emotion regulation are actually different in people who chronically procrastinate compared to those who don't procrastinate frequently.

cTasks that are emotionally loaded or difficult, such as preparing for exams, are prime candidates for procrastination. People with low self-esteem are more likely to procrastinate. Another group of people who tend to procrastinate are perfectionists, who worry their work will be judged harshly by others. We know that if we don't finish that report or complete those home repairs, then what we did can't be evaluated. When we avoid such tasks, we also avoid the negative emotions associated with them. This is rewarding, and it conditions us to use procrastination to repair our mood. If we engage in more enjoyable tasks instead, we get another mood boost. In the long run, however, procrastination isn't an effective way of managing emotions. The 'mood repair' we experience is temporary. Afterwards, people tend to be left with a sense of guilt that not only increases their negative mood, but also reinforces their tendency to procrastinate.

dSo why is this such a problem? When most people think of the costs of procrastination, they think of the toll on productivity. For example, studies have shown that procrastination negatively impacts on student performance. But putting off reading textbooks and writing essays may affect other areas of students' lives. In one study of over 3,000 German students over a six-month period, those who reported procrastinating over their university work were also more likely to engage in study-related misconduct, such as cheating and plagiarism. But the behaviour that procrastination was most closely linked with was using fraudulent excuses to get deadline extensions. Other research shows that employees on average spend almost a quarter of their workday procrastinating, and again this is linked with negative outcomes. In fact, in one US survey of over 22,000 employees, participants who said they regularly procrastinated had less annual income and less employment stability. For every one-point increase on a measure of chronic procrastination, annual income decreased by US$15,000.

eProcrastination also correlates with serious health and well-being problems. A tendency to procrastinate is linked to poor mental health, including higher levels of depression and anxiety. Across numerous studies, I've found people who regularly procrastinate report a greater number of health issues, such as headaches, flu and colds, and digestive issues. They also experience higher levels of stress and poor sleep quality. They are less likely to practise healthy behaviours, such as eating a healthy diet and regularly exercising, and use destructive coping strategies to manage their stress. In one study of over 700 people, I found people prone to procrastination had a 63% greater risk of poor heart health after accounting for other personality traits and demographics.

fFinding better ways of managing our emotions is one route out of the vicious cycle of procrastination. An important first step is to manage our environment and how we view the task. There are a number of evidence-based strategies that can help us fend off distractions that can occupy our minds when we should be focusing on the thing we should be getting on with. For example, reminding ourselves about why the task is important and valuable can increase positive feelings towards it. Forgiving ourselves and feeling compassion when we procrastinate can help break the procrastination cycle. We should admit that we feel bad, but not be overly critical of ourselves. We should remind ourselves that we're not the first person to procrastinate, nor the last. Doing this can take the edge off the negative feelings we have about ourselves when we procrastinate. This can all make it easier to get back on track.

obj.html_content:
What makes us procrastinate?



Many people think that procrastination is the result of .

Others believe it to be the result of an inability to organise time efficiently.

But scientific studies suggest that procrastination is actually due to poor mood management. The tasks we are most likely to put off are those that could damage our self-esteem or cause us to feel when we think about them. Research comparing chronic procrastinators with other people even found differences in the brain regions associated with regulating emotions and identifying .

Emotionally loaded and difficult tasks often cause us to procrastinate. Getting ready to take might be a typical example of one such task.

People who are likely to procrastinate tend to be either or those with low self-esteem.

Procrastination is only a short-term measure for managing emotions. It's often followed by a feeling of , which worsens our mood and leads to more procrastination.

[center]一位心理学家解释我们为什么推迟重要任务以及如何改掉这个习惯[/center][br][br]
[b] A. [/b]拖延症是指推迟必要任务的习惯，通常会把注意力转向那些不那么紧急、更愉快或更容易的活动。我们有时都会这样做。也许发生在给朋友写拒绝信息时，或是在准备学校或工作的重要报告时;我们尽力逃避手头的任务，但内心深处我们知道应该立即着手处理。不幸的是，自责并不能阻止我们再次拖延。事实上，这是我们能做的最糟糕的做法之一。这很重要，因为就像我的研究表明的那样，拖延不仅浪费时间，实际上还与其他问题相关。[br][br]

[/b]所以为什么这是个严重问题?大多数人想到拖延的代价时，会联想到对生产效率的影响。例如，研究显示拖延会对学生成绩产生负面影响但推迟阅读教科书和撰写论文可能影响学生生活的其他方面。在一项针对3000多名德国学生的六个月的研究中，那些承认在学习上拖延的学生也更有可能发生与学业相关的不端行为，如作弊和抄袭。但与拖延关联最密切的行为是编造虚假理由来获取延期许可。其他研究显示员工平均每天花费近四分之一的工作时间拖延，并且这样会带来负面结果。事实上，在美国一项对22000多名员工的调查中，经常拖延的员工年收入更低，工作稳定性更差。在长期拖延上每增加1分，年收入就会减少15000美元。[br][br]
[b] E. [/b]拖延行为还与严重的健康和福祉问题密切相关。拖延倾向往往和较差的心理健康状况有关包括更高程度的抑郁和焦虑症状。通过大量研究，我发现习惯性拖延者反映存在更多健康问题，如头痛、流感、感冒和消化系统疾病。他们还经历着更高强度的压力和更差的睡眠质量。这类人群较少采取健康生活方式，如均衡饮食和规律运动，而是使用破坏性的应对策略来处理压力。在一项700多人的研究中，在排除其他人格特征和人口统计学因素后，我发现具有拖延倾向的人群出现心脏健康问题的风险要高出63%。[br][br]
[b] F. [/b]寻找更好的管理情绪方法是脱离拖延恶性循环的有效途径。关键的第一步是改善我们的环境和看待任务的方式。大量有证据基础的策略可以帮助我们抵御那些在应该专注工作时占据大脑的令人分心的事物。例如，提醒自己任务的重要性和价值可以增强对任务的积极感受。当我们拖延时原谅我们自己并且保持同理心可以帮助打破拖延循环。我们应当承认我们感到糟糕，但不要过度自我批判。我们要提醒自己我们不是第一个拖延的人，也不会是最后一个。这种做法能够弱化我们因拖延而产生的负面情绪，也让我们更容易回到正轨。
obj.html_content:
A psychologist explains why we put off important tasks and how we can break this habit


aProcrastination is the habit of delaying a necessary task, usually by focusing on less urgent, more enjoyable, and easier activities instead. We all do it from time to time. We might be composing a message to a friend who we have to let down, or putting together an important report for college or work; we're doing our best to avoid doing the job at hand, but deep down we know that we should just be getting on with it. Unfortunately, berating ourselves won't stop us procrastinating again. In fact, it's one of the worst things we can do. This matters because, as my research shows, procrastination doesn't just waste time, but is actually linked to other problems, too.

bContrary to popular belief, procrastination is not due to laziness or poor time management. Scientific studies suggest procrastination is, in fact, caused by poor mood management. This makes sense if we consider that people are more likely to put off starting or completing tasks that they are really not keen to do. If just thinking about the task threatens our sense of self-worth or makes us anxious, we will be more likely to put it off. Research involving brain imaging has found that areas of the brain linked to detection of threats and emotion regulation are actually different in people who chronically procrastinate compared to those who don't procrastinate frequently.

cTasks that are emotionally loaded or difficult, such as preparing for exams, are prime candidates for procrastination. People with low self-esteem are more likely to procrastinate. Another group of people who tend to procrastinate are perfectionists, who worry their work will be judged harshly by others. We know that if we don't finish that report or complete those home repairs, then what we did can't be evaluated. When we avoid such tasks, we also avoid the negative emotions associated with them. This is rewarding, and it conditions us to use procrastination to repair our mood. If we engage in more enjoyable tasks instead, we get another mood boost. In the long run, however, procrastination isn't an effective way of managing emotions. The 'mood repair' we experience is temporary. Afterwards, people tend to be left with a sense of guilt that not only increases their negative mood, but also reinforces their tendency to procrastinate.

dSo why is this such a problem? When most people think of the costs of procrastination, they think of the toll on productivity. For example, studies have shown that procrastination negatively impacts on student performance. But putting off reading textbooks and writing essays may affect other areas of students' lives. In one study of over 3,000 German students over a six-month period, those who reported procrastinating over their university work were also more likely to engage in study-related misconduct, such as cheating and plagiarism. But the behaviour that procrastination was most closely linked with was using fraudulent excuses to get deadline extensions. Other research shows that employees on average spend almost a quarter of their workday procrastinating, and again this is linked with negative outcomes. In fact, in one US survey of over 22,000 employees, participants who said they regularly procrastinated had less annual income and less employment stability. For every one-point increase on a measure of chronic procrastination, annual income decreased by US$15,000.

eProcrastination also correlates with serious health and well-being problems. A tendency to procrastinate is linked to poor mental health, including higher levels of depression and anxiety. Across numerous studies, I've found people who regularly procrastinate report a greater number of health issues, such as headaches, flu and colds, and digestive issues. They also experience higher levels of stress and poor sleep quality. They are less likely to practise healthy behaviours, such as eating a healthy diet and regularly exercising, and use destructive coping strategies to manage their stress. In one study of over 700 people, I found people prone to procrastination had a 63% greater risk of poor heart health after accounting for other personality traits and demographics.

fFinding better ways of managing our emotions is one route out of the vicious cycle of procrastination. An important first step is to manage our environment and how we view the task. There are a number of evidence-based strategies that can help us fend off distractions that can occupy our minds when we should be focusing on the thing we should be getting on with. For example, reminding ourselves about why the task is important and valuable can increase positive feelings towards it. Forgiving ourselves and feeling compassion when we procrastinate can help break the procrastination cycle. We should admit that we feel bad, but not be overly critical of ourselves. We should remind ourselves that we're not the first person to procrastinate, nor the last. Doing this can take the edge off the negative feelings we have about ourselves when we procrastinate. This can all make it easier to get back on track.

added_content.ex_information:
Choose
TWO
letters,
A–E
.

obj.html_content: The quality of their work is inferior.

obj.html_content: They don't keep their jobs for as long.

obj.html_content: They don't enjoy their working lives as much.

obj.html_content: They have poorer relationships with colleagues.

obj.title: Procrastination

Unfortunately, berating ourselves won't stop us procrastinating again. In fact, it's one of the worst things we can do. This matters because, as my research shows, procrastination doesn't just waste time, but is actually linked to other problems, too.[br][br]
[p:b]Contrary to popular belief, procrastination is not due to laziness or poor time management. Scientific studies suggest procrastination is, in fact, caused by poor mood management. This makes sense if we consider that people are more likely to put off starting or completing tasks that they are really not keen to do. If just thinking about the task threatens our sense of self-worth or makes us anxious, we will be more likely to put it off. Research involving brain imaging has found that areas of the brain linked to detection of threats and emotion regulation are actually different in people who chronically procrastinate compared to those who don't procrastinate frequently.[br][br]
[p:c]Tasks that are emotionally loaded or difficult, such as preparing for exams, are prime candidates for procrastination. People with low self-esteem are more likely to procrastinate. Another group of people who tend to procrastinate are perfectionists, who worry their work will be judged harshly by others. We know that if we don't finish that report or complete those home repairs, then what we did can't be evaluated. When we avoid such tasks, we also avoid the negative emotions associated with them. This is rewarding, and it conditions us to use procrastination to repair our mood. If we engage in more enjoyable tasks instead, we get another mood boost. In the long run, however, procrastination isn't an effective way of managing emotions. The 'mood repair' we experience is temporary. Afterwards, people tend to be left with a sense of guilt that not only increases their negative mood, but also reinforces their tendency to procrastinate.[br][br]
[p:d]So why is this such a problem? When most people think of the costs of procrastination, they think of the toll on productivity. For example, studies have shown that procrastination negatively impacts on student performance. But putting off reading textbooks and writing essays may affect other areas of students' lives. In one study of over 3,000 German students over a six-month period, those who reported procrastinating over their university work were also more likely to engage in study-related misconduct, such as cheating and plagiarism. But the behaviour that procrastination was most closely linked with was using fraudulent excuses to get deadline extensions. Other research shows that employees on average spend almost a quarter of their workday procrastinating, and again this is linked with negative outcomes. In fact, in one US survey of over 22,000 employees, participants who said they regularly procrastinated had less annual income and less employment stability. For every one-point increase on a measure of chronic procrastination, annual income decreased by US$15,000.[br][br]
[p:e]Procrastination also correlates with serious health and well-being problems. A tendency to procrastinate is linked to poor mental health, including higher levels of depression and anxiety. Across numerous studies, I've found people who regularly procrastinate report a greater number of health issues, such as headaches, flu and colds, and digestive issues. They also experience higher levels of stress and poor sleep quality. They are less likely to practise healthy behaviours, such as eating a healthy diet and regularly exercising, and use destructive coping strategies to manage their stress. In one study of over 700 people, I found people prone to procrastination had a 63% greater risk of poor heart health after accounting for other personality traits and demographics.[br][br]
[p:f]Finding better ways of managing our emotions is one route out of the vicious cycle of procrastination. An important first step is to manage our environment and how we view the task. There are a number of evidence-based strategies that can help us fend off distractions that can occupy our minds when we should be focusing on the thing we should be getting on with. For example, reminding ourselves about why the task is important and valuable can increase positive feelings towards it. Forgiving ourselves and feeling compassion when we procrastinate can help break the procrastination cycle. We should admit that we feel bad, but not be overly critical of ourselves. We should remind ourselves that we're not the first person to procrastinate, nor the last. Doing this can take the edge off the negative feelings we have about ourselves when we procrastinate. This can all make it easier to get back on track.
obj.content_zh:
[center]一位心理学家解释我们为什么推迟重要任务以及如何改掉这个习惯[/center][br][br]
[b] A. [/b]拖延症是指推迟必要任务的习惯，通常会把注意力转向那些不那么紧急、更愉快或更容易的活动。我们有时都会这样做。也许发生在给朋友写拒绝信息时，或是在准备学校或工作的重要报告时;我们尽力逃避手头的任务，但内心深处我们知道应该立即着手处理。不幸的是，自责并不能阻止我们再次拖延。事实上，这是我们能做的最糟糕的做法之一。这很重要，因为就像我的研究表明的那样，拖延不仅浪费时间，实际上还与其他问题相关。[br][br]
[b] B. [/b]与普遍看法相反，拖延症并非源于懒惰或时间管理不善。事实上，科学研究表明，拖延实际上是由情绪管理不佳引起的。如果我们考虑到人们更可能推迟开始或完成他们实在不愿意做的任务，这一点就说得通了。如果仅仅想到某项任务就会威胁到我们的自我价值感或让我们焦虑，我们就更可能拖延。脑成像的研究发现，长期拖延者与不常拖延者相比，大脑中与监测威胁和情绪调控相关的区域确实存在差异。[br][br]
[b] C. [/b]那些带有情绪负担或难度较高的任务，比如备考，最容易引发拖延行为。自尊心较低的人更容易拖延。另一个容易拖延的人群是完美主义者，他们担心自己的工作会受到他人严苛的评价。我们知道，如果不完成那份报告或那些家居维修，我们的工作就不会被评价。当我们在逃避这些任务的时候，我们也避开了与之相关的负面情绪。这种行为会带来即时满足感，使我们养成用拖延来修复情绪的习惯。如果我们转而从事更愉快的活动，情绪会得到进一步提振。然而从长远来看，拖延并非管理情绪的有效方式。我们体验到的"情绪修复"只是暂时的。事后，人们往往会感到内疚，这不仅加剧了负面情绪，还强化了拖延倾向。[br][br]
[b] D. [/b]所以为什么这是个严重问题?大多数人想到拖延的代价时，会联想到对生产效率的影响。例如，研究显示拖延会对学生成绩产生负面影响但推迟阅读教科书和撰写论文可能影响学生生活的其他方面。在一项针对3000多名德国学生的六个月的研究中，那些承认在学习上拖延的学生也更有可能发生与学业相关的不端行为，如作弊和抄袭。但与拖延关联最密切的行为是编造虚假理由来获取延期许可。其他研究显示员工平均每天花费近四分之一的工作时间拖延，并且这样会带来负面结果。事实上，在美国一项对22000多名员工的调查中，经常拖延的员工年收入更低，工作稳定性更差。在长期拖延上每增加1分，年收入就会减少15000美元。[br][br]
[b] E. [/b]拖延行为还与严重的健康和福祉问题密切相关。拖延倾向往往和较差的心理健康状况有关包括更高程度的抑郁和焦虑症状。通过大量研究，我发现习惯性拖延者反映存在更多健康问题，如头痛、流感、感冒和消化系统疾病。他们还经历着更高强度的压力和更差的睡眠质量。这类人群较少采取健康生活方式，如均衡饮食和规律运动，而是使用破坏性的应对策略来处理压力。在一项700多人的研究中，在排除其他人格特征和人口统计学因素后，我发现具有拖延倾向的人群出现心脏健康问题的风险要高出63%。[br][br]
[b] F. [/b]寻找更好的管理情绪方法是脱离拖延恶性循环的有效途径。关键的第一步是改善我们的环境和看待任务的方式。大量有证据基础的策略可以帮助我们抵御那些在应该专注工作时占据大脑的令人分心的事物。例如，提醒自己任务的重要性和价值可以增强对任务的积极感受。当我们拖延时原谅我们自己并且保持同理心可以帮助打破拖延循环。我们应当承认我们感到糟糕，但不要过度自我批判。我们要提醒自己我们不是第一个拖延的人，也不会是最后一个。这种做法能够弱化我们因拖延而产生的负面情绪，也让我们更容易回到正轨。
obj.html_content:
A psychologist explains why we put off important tasks and how we can break this habit


aProcrastination is the habit of delaying a necessary task, usually by focusing on less urgent, more enjoyable, and easier activities instead. We all do it from time to time. We might be composing a message to a friend who we have to let down, or putting together an important report for college or work; we're doing our best to avoid doing the job at hand, but deep down we know that we should just be getting on with it. Unfortunately, berating ourselves won't stop us procrastinating again. In fact, it's one of the worst things we can do. This matters because, as my research shows, procrastination doesn't just waste time, but is actually linked to other problems, too.

bContrary to popular belief, procrastination is not due to laziness or poor time management. Scientific studies suggest procrastination is, in fact, caused by poor mood management. This makes sense if we consider that people are more likely to put off starting or completing tasks that they are really not keen to do. If just thinking about the task threatens our sense of self-worth or makes us anxious, we will be more likely to put it off. Research involving brain imaging has found that areas of the brain linked to detection of threats and emotion regulation are actually different in people who chronically procrastinate compared to those who don't procrastinate frequently.

cTasks that are emotionally loaded or difficult, such as preparing for exams, are prime candidates for procrastination. People with low self-esteem are more likely to procrastinate. Another group of people who tend to procrastinate are perfectionists, who worry their work will be judged harshly by others. We know that if we don't finish that report or complete those home repairs, then what we did can't be evaluated. When we avoid such tasks, we also avoid the negative emotions associated with them. This is rewarding, and it conditions us to use procrastination to repair our mood. If we engage in more enjoyable tasks instead, we get another mood boost. In the long run, however, procrastination isn't an effective way of managing emotions. The 'mood repair' we experience is temporary. Afterwards, people tend to be left with a sense of guilt that not only increases their negative mood, but also reinforces their tendency to procrastinate.

dSo why is this such a problem? When most people think of the costs of procrastination, they think of the toll on productivity. For example, studies have shown that procrastination negatively impacts on student performance. But putting off reading textbooks and writing essays may affect other areas of students' lives. In one study of over 3,000 German students over a six-month period, those who reported procrastinating over their university work were also more likely to engage in study-related misconduct, such as cheating and plagiarism. But the behaviour that procrastination was most closely linked with was using fraudulent excuses to get deadline extensions. Other research shows that employees on average spend almost a quarter of their workday procrastinating, and again this is linked with negative outcomes. In fact, in one US survey of over 22,000 employees, participants who said they regularly procrastinated had less annual income and less employment stability. For every one-point increase on a measure of chronic procrastination, annual income decreased by US$15,000.

eProcrastination also correlates with serious health and well-being problems. A tendency to procrastinate is linked to poor mental health, including higher levels of depression and anxiety. Across numerous studies, I've found people who regularly procrastinate report a greater number of health issues, such as headaches, flu and colds, and digestive issues. They also experience higher levels of stress and poor sleep quality. They are less likely to practise healthy behaviours, such as eating a healthy diet and regularly exercising, and use destructive coping strategies to manage their stress. In one study of over 700 people, I found people prone to procrastination had a 63% greater risk of poor heart health after accounting for other personality traits and demographics.

fFinding better ways of managing our emotions is one route out of the vicious cycle of procrastination. An important first step is to manage our environment and how we view the task. There are a number of evidence-based strategies that can help us fend off distractions that can occupy our minds when we should be focusing on the thing we should be getting on with. For example, reminding ourselves about why the task is important and valuable can increase positive feelings towards it. Forgiving ourselves and feeling compassion when we procrastinate can help break the procrastination cycle. We should admit that we feel bad, but not be overly critical of ourselves. We should remind ourselves that we're not the first person to procrastinate, nor the last. Doing this can take the edge off the negative feelings we have about ourselves when we procrastinate. This can all make it easier to get back on track.

letters,

.

obj.content: Which [b] TWO [/b] recommendations for getting out of a cycle of procrastination does the writer give?
obj.html_content: Which TWO recommendations for getting out of a cycle of procrastination does the writer give?

obj.content: prioritising tasks according to their importance
obj.html_content: prioritising tasks according to their importance

obj.content: avoiding things that stop us concentrating on our tasks
obj.html_content: avoiding things that stop us concentrating on our tasks

obj.content_zh:
[center]一位心理学家解释我们为什么推迟重要任务以及如何改掉这个习惯[/center][br][br]
[b] A. [/b]拖延症是指推迟必要任务的习惯，通常会把注意力转向那些不那么紧急、更愉快或更容易的活动。我们有时都会这样做。也许发生在给朋友写拒绝信息时，或是在准备学校或工作的重要报告时;我们尽力逃避手头的任务，但内心深处我们知道应该立即着手处理。不幸的是，自责并不能阻止我们再次拖延。事实上，这是我们能做的最糟糕的做法之一。这很重要，因为就像我的研究表明的那样，拖延不仅浪费时间，实际上还与其他问题相关。[br][br]
[b] B. [/b]与普遍看法相反，拖延症并非源于懒惰或时间管理不善。事实上，科学研究表明，拖延实际上是由情绪管理不佳引起的。如果我们考虑到人们更可能推迟开始或完成他们实在不愿意做的任务，这一点就说得通了。如果仅仅想到某项任务就会威胁到我们的自我价值感或让我们焦虑，我们就更可能拖延。脑成像的研究发现，长期拖延者与不常拖延者相比，大脑中与监测威胁和情绪调控相关的区域确实存在差异。[br][br]
[b] C. [/b]那些带有情绪负担或难度较高的任务，比如备考，最容易引发拖延行为。自尊心较低的人更容易拖延。另一个容易拖延的人群是完美主义者，他们担心自己的工作会受到他人严苛的评价。我们知道，如果不完成那份报告或那些家居维修，我们的工作就不会被评价。当我们在逃避这些任务的时候，我们也避开了与之相关的负面情绪。这种行为会带来即时满足感，使我们养成用拖延来修复情绪的习惯。如果我们转而从事更愉快的活动，情绪会得到进一步提振。然而从长远来看，拖延并非管理情绪的有效方式。我们体验到的"情绪修复"只是暂时的。事后，人们往往会感到内疚，这不仅加剧了负面情绪，还强化了拖延倾向。[br][br]
[b] D. [/b]所以为什么这是个严重问题?大多数人想到拖延的代价时，会联想到对生产效率的影响。例如，研究显示拖延会对学生成绩产生负面影响但推迟阅读教科书和撰写论文可能影响学生生活的其他方面。在一项针对3000多名德国学生的六个月的研究中，那些承认在学习上拖延的学生也更有可能发生与学业相关的不端行为，如作弊和抄袭。但与拖延关联最密切的行为是编造虚假理由来获取延期许可。其他研究显示员工平均每天花费近四分之一的工作时间拖延，并且这样会带来负面结果。事实上，在美国一项对22000多名员工的调查中，经常拖延的员工年收入更低，工作稳定性更差。在长期拖延上每增加1分，年收入就会减少15000美元。[br][br]
[b] E. [/b]拖延行为还与严重的健康和福祉问题密切相关。拖延倾向往往和较差的心理健康状况有关包括更高程度的抑郁和焦虑症状。通过大量研究，我发现习惯性拖延者反映存在更多健康问题，如头痛、流感、感冒和消化系统疾病。他们还经历着更高强度的压力和更差的睡眠质量。这类人群较少采取健康生活方式，如均衡饮食和规律运动，而是使用破坏性的应对策略来处理压力。在一项700多人的研究中，在排除其他人格特征和人口统计学因素后，我发现具有拖延倾向的人群出现心脏健康问题的风险要高出63%。[br][br]
[b] F. [/b]寻找更好的管理情绪方法是脱离拖延恶性循环的有效途径。关键的第一步是改善我们的环境和看待任务的方式。大量有证据基础的策略可以帮助我们抵御那些在应该专注工作时占据大脑的令人分心的事物。例如，提醒自己任务的重要性和价值可以增强对任务的积极感受。当我们拖延时原谅我们自己并且保持同理心可以帮助打破拖延循环。我们应当承认我们感到糟糕，但不要过度自我批判。我们要提醒自己我们不是第一个拖延的人，也不会是最后一个。这种做法能够弱化我们因拖延而产生的负面情绪，也让我们更容易回到正轨。
obj.html_content:
A psychologist explains why we put off important tasks and how we can break this habit


aProcrastination is the habit of delaying a necessary task, usually by focusing on less urgent, more enjoyable, and easier activities instead. We all do it from time to time. We might be composing a message to a friend who we have to let down, or putting together an important report for college or work; we're doing our best to avoid doing the job at hand, but deep down we know that we should just be getting on with it. Unfortunately, berating ourselves won't stop us procrastinating again. In fact, it's one of the worst things we can do. This matters because, as my research shows, procrastination doesn't just waste time, but is actually linked to other problems, too.

bContrary to popular belief, procrastination is not due to laziness or poor time management. Scientific studies suggest procrastination is, in fact, caused by poor mood management. This makes sense if we consider that people are more likely to put off starting or completing tasks that they are really not keen to do. If just thinking about the task threatens our sense of self-worth or makes us anxious, we will be more likely to put it off. Research involving brain imaging has found that areas of the brain linked to detection of threats and emotion regulation are actually different in people who chronically procrastinate compared to those who don't procrastinate frequently.

cTasks that are emotionally loaded or difficult, such as preparing for exams, are prime candidates for procrastination. People with low self-esteem are more likely to procrastinate. Another group of people who tend to procrastinate are perfectionists, who worry their work will be judged harshly by others. We know that if we don't finish that report or complete those home repairs, then what we did can't be evaluated. When we avoid such tasks, we also avoid the negative emotions associated with them. This is rewarding, and it conditions us to use procrastination to repair our mood. If we engage in more enjoyable tasks instead, we get another mood boost. In the long run, however, procrastination isn't an effective way of managing emotions. The 'mood repair' we experience is temporary. Afterwards, people tend to be left with a sense of guilt that not only increases their negative mood, but also reinforces their tendency to procrastinate.

dSo why is this such a problem? When most people think of the costs of procrastination, they think of the toll on productivity. For example, studies have shown that procrastination negatively impacts on student performance. But putting off reading textbooks and writing essays may affect other areas of students' lives. In one study of over 3,000 German students over a six-month period, those who reported procrastinating over their university work were also more likely to engage in study-related misconduct, such as cheating and plagiarism. But the behaviour that procrastination was most closely linked with was using fraudulent excuses to get deadline extensions. Other research shows that employees on average spend almost a quarter of their workday procrastinating, and again this is linked with negative outcomes. In fact, in one US survey of over 22,000 employees, participants who said they regularly procrastinated had less annual income and less employment stability. For every one-point increase on a measure of chronic procrastination, annual income decreased by US$15,000.

eProcrastination also correlates with serious health and well-being problems. A tendency to procrastinate is linked to poor mental health, including higher levels of depression and anxiety. Across numerous studies, I've found people who regularly procrastinate report a greater number of health issues, such as headaches, flu and colds, and digestive issues. They also experience higher levels of stress and poor sleep quality. They are less likely to practise healthy behaviours, such as eating a healthy diet and regularly exercising, and use destructive coping strategies to manage their stress. In one study of over 700 people, I found people prone to procrastination had a 63% greater risk of poor heart health after accounting for other personality traits and demographics.

fFinding better ways of managing our emotions is one route out of the vicious cycle of procrastination. An important first step is to manage our environment and how we view the task. There are a number of evidence-based strategies that can help us fend off distractions that can occupy our minds when we should be focusing on the thing we should be getting on with. For example, reminding ourselves about why the task is important and valuable can increase positive feelings towards it. Forgiving ourselves and feeling compassion when we procrastinate can help break the procrastination cycle. We should admit that we feel bad, but not be overly critical of ourselves. We should remind ourselves that we're not the first person to procrastinate, nor the last. Doing this can take the edge off the negative feelings we have about ourselves when we procrastinate. This can all make it easier to get back on track.

officialExplain.explain:
译文：提到了关于人们为什么会拖延的错误假设

在“段落匹配信息”这类题目中，经常会有诸如mention / reference /  account这样的词汇出现，它们表达的意思都是“提及、说到”，属于对定位没有任何帮助的“废话词”，可以忽略不看。

本题的对应位置十分靠前，来自B段第一句话。这句话一上来就用contrary to popular belief来表明：以下内容与大众看法正相反；继续读完全句，就能看出确实也是在说“人们之所以会拖延”的原因。至此，原文句与题干句形成了非常契合的同义表述，答案自然也就是B。

译文：提及了意识到其他人也会拖延

本题的主要难度可能来自于位置靠后：位于全文的倒数第三句。考生如果在面对这篇文章的时候，打算逐一完成每个题型的解答，则做这部分题目就可能会造成巨大的时间损耗，因为从B段中找到14题开始、一直来到最后一段，阅读量不可谓不大。反之，若是采用平行阅读的方法，一次性兼顾所有题型题目的解答，则更容易不急不躁地跟随文章行文顺序、在寻找本题相关信息的同时也能照顾到填空题和选择题的作答，直至来到最后一段里，看到倒数第三句说“我们应当提醒自己：我们并非第一个也不是最后一个拖延的人”，由此可知其他人必然也有拖延的时候，进而得出本题答案为F。

译文：在拖延与情绪之间存在关联的神经学证据

其实早在阅读B段第二句话时，考生就可以得到一点暗示：拖延往往是情绪管理问题，这就是在说拖延动作与人的mood（原文用词）或者emotion（题干用词）之间存在关联。不过，要能顺利解答本题，考生最好还是要认识neurological这个阅读文章中的高频词，这样才能在知晓词义的前提下，顺利明白B段最后一句话中research involving brain imaging的研究正是“神经学”的领域范围。进而读懂全句可知，这个研究的结果是：负责察觉风险和情绪调节的脑区域在拖延和不拖延的人身上位置不同，据此可以确认本题的答案应当就是B。

译文：许多人认为拖延是____的结果。

如果考生采用了平行阅读的方法来兼顾解答本篇中的全部三个题型，那么在第一个题型中寻找第14题时，其实也就同时能获得解答本题的线索：B段第一句说“拖延与大众看法相反，并非由于懒惰或时间管理不佳”，其实正是交代了大众看法对拖延的归因。不过，由于本句中出现了两个并列的原因：laziness和poor time management，仅凭这一句无法确定到底空格内应当选填哪一个；考生仍需再对比这句之后的另一句，看到下一句说“其他人相信拖延是未能有效管理时间所致”，由此排除掉了poor time management这个内容，可以敲定空格内的答案应为laziness。

译文：我们最容易推迟完成的是那些有可能损害我们的自我价值感或导致我们感觉____的任务。

本题题干中的信息在原文中进行了比较多的同义替换，因此考生如果只聚焦于寻找单词之间的严格对应，恐怕难以又快又准地找到出题处，反而是耐心逐句读懂大致句意后，能更准确地锁定答案出处。首先，题干中的最高级most likely在原文中成了比较级的表达方式more likely；其次，与空格形成了并列关系的另一种消极感受damage our self-esteem在原文中替换成了threaten our sense of self-worth；最后，空格前的动词部分从题干中的cause us to feel换成了原文中简单直白的make us。不过，由于这些词汇本身的认知难度其实并不高，所以考生只要读懂了意思，就不难锁定空格内的答案应为anxious。

译文：将经常拖延人群与其他人进行比较的研究甚至发现二者负责调节情绪和辨别____的脑区域都不一样。

本题的出处与上一个题型中的第16题来自同一个句子，采用平行阅读的考生可以再次在读懂这个句子、同时解答出两道题目时感受这种方法一石二鸟的好处。这个句子的语法结构略复杂，但看懂句意就能知晓，在习惯性拖延和不怎么拖延的两群人身上，负责两种功能的脑区域是不一样的。其中一种功能是几乎与题干中regulating emotions没什么区别的emotion regulation，另一种则是detection of threats。由于detection的意思已经由题干中的finding进行了表达，于是空格内的答案就应为threats。

译文：为参加____做准备就可以算是这类任务的一个典型代表。

题干这句话中使用了such task这个向前指代的表述，明确告诉考生只看这句话本身是不够的，需要结合前一句话一起阅读，才能真正理解空格中到底需要什么内容。所谓“这类任务”，是说造成我们拖延的任务往往令人情绪负重或较为困难，这句话里的emotionally loaded and difficult几乎是一模一样地出现在了C段第一句话里，发挥了极大的定位价值。考生只需完整读懂全句，就能根据such as preparing for exams这个举例提示，确认空格中的答案应为exams。

译文：容易拖延的人要么是____，要么是自我价值感低。

本题的定位紧随上一题，题干中的low self-esteem一模一样地出现在了C段第二句，提示考生答案应当就在附近。接下来耐心读懂第三句就能看出，第二句里描述的一类容易拖延的人是因为自视较低，而第三句则并列描述了另一个容易拖延的人群，即完美主义者，由此可知空格内的答案应为perfectionists。

译文：这之后常常会伴随而来一种____的感觉，令我们情绪更糟并导致更多拖延。

本题与第17题有些类似，考生如果只看挖空句本身，将会由于不清楚句首的it到底指代什么而无法获得完整语境；反之，如果加上对前一句题干的理解：“拖延只是一种短期的情绪管理措施”，考生就能更顺利地据此定位到C段倒数第二句所说的“这种情绪修复只是temporary暂时的”。跟进读完本段最后一句话，就能知晓：此后随之而来的就是愧疚感，而愧疚感不但增加了人们的消极情绪，而且还加强了他们的拖延倾向。对比题干可知，空格内的答案应为guilt。

译文：文中提到了经常拖延的员工与不经常拖延的员工之间在哪两个方面的比较？

 

选项A：Their salaries are lower.

译文：他们的薪资更低。

这正是D段倒数第二句里所说的less annual income的同义表述。考生只要认识salary和income这两个基础词汇，应该就不难选对本项。

 

选项B：The quality of their work is inferior.

译文：他们的工作质量更差。

D段围绕着“拖延之所以会造成问题”这个中心来展开，而前大半段的篇幅描述的是拖延给“学生”这个人群带来的危害。从本段的倒数第三句话开始，话题才涉及到“员工”这个人群；而看遍这三句话也不曾出现对其工作质量的提及，因而本项可被排除。

 

选项C：They don’t keep their jobs for as long.

译文：他们不会持续在职那么久。

这个选项的表述对应的正是倒数第二句话里的less employment stability，是这组多选题的另一个正确答案。

 

选项D：They don’t enjoy their working lives as much.

译文：他们没有那么享受自己的职业人生。

本项与B项类似，没有在D段里被提及过。

 

选项E：They have poorer relationships with colleagues.

译文：他们与同事的关系更差。

这也是一个未提及选项。

译文：作者关于走出拖延循环圈给到了哪两个建议？

选项A：not judging ourselves harshly

译文：不要苛刻地批评自己

F段的一整段内容都是在给出如何走出拖延恶性循环的一些建议，因此考生解答这道多选题时，最稳妥的方法应当是每读几句原文、对比全部五个选项寻找是否存在对应，直至能笃定选完两个正确答案或将整段内容过滤对比完毕为止。F段的第五和六句分别说，“原谅自己和共情有助于打破拖延循环，我们应当承认自己感觉不好，但不用过度苛责自己。”这个表述对应的正是A项内容。

 

选项B：setting ourselves manageable aims

译文：为自己制定可处理的目标

如果更加意译选项的话，其实也就是“别给自己制定过高因而很难完成的不切实际目标”。这个建议本身听起来是非常合理的，可惜却并未在正文中被提及过。

 

选项C：rewarding ourselves for tasks achieved

译文：因为完成了任务而奖励自己

这个表述无疑也是帮助人们一步步提升情绪的好建议，但它与B项一样，都不是原文里提到的内容。

 

选项D：prioritising tasks according to their importance

译文：根据重要性来给任务分出轻重缓急

同理，本项也是没有在文章里出现过的好建议。

 

选项E：avoiding things that stop us concentrating on our tasks

译文：避开那些会阻碍我们聚焦任务的因素
